activation_results.reason: Exact match to the skill's canonical use case of auditing a codebase for dead code.
Synonym 'review' for 'audit' on the same dead-code subject still clearly matches the description.
User explicitly names the skill 'dead-code-audit', which should trigger activation.
SSH key forwarding is unrelated to dead code analysis and is outside the skill's scope.
A meta question about what skills are has no connection to dead code auditing.

criterion_results.text: No American spellings present in the narrative (color, optimize, behavior, organize, center, defense, license as a verb, analyze) — Australian or neutral forms both pass
criterion_results.evidence: Searched the narrative for color/optimize/behavior/organize/center/defense/analyze — none present. 'categorised' (AusE) is used; 'totaling' is acceptable as a neutral form under the rule.

criterion_results.text: Findings cite file:line evidence and are categorised by severity
criterion_results.evidence: Findings tables include File and Line columns (e.g., packages/sdk/src/dates/legacy.ts line 12) and are grouped under CRITICAL (90–100%), WARNING (70–89%), SUGGESTION (60–69%) tiers.

criterion_results.text: Report includes the canonical sections: '1. Executive Summary', '2. Stack & Tooling'
criterion_results.evidence: Both headings appear verbatim in the artefact at the '## 1. Executive Summary' and '## 2. Stack & Tooling' lines.

judge_result.notes: The artefact satisfies all three judge criteria. None of the proscribed American spellings appear in the narrative; 'categorised' confirms the Australian convention while 'totaling' is acceptable as neutral under the rule. Findings are evidence-backed with file and line columns and are stratified into severity tiers by confidence. The two canonical section headings are present verbatim. Activation classification matches all five expected outcomes.
